FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=7691, baseline ages 9-10 years, Years 2-4 follow-up data (2018-2022), 21 recruitment sites across U.S.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis using generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) to estimate mediation pathways; sequential modeling approaches with direct and indirect effects quantified

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Drug and Alcohol Dependence 277(2025):112947

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (IV, Year 2) paired with cannabis experimentation (DV, Year 4); cannabis experimentation is an individual-trait-level behavioral outcome. Social media measured as daily hours (self-report, ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey)

FT_IC3.evidence: Direct effect B=0.18 (95% CI 0.13-0.24, p<0.001); indirect effects via positive expectancies B=0.05 (95% CI 0.03-0.06, p<0.001) and negative expectancies B=0.03 (95% CI 0.02-0.04, p<0.001); mediation percentages 19.8% and 13.6%

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantification across multiple pathways: direct effect, indirect effects (positive and negative), confidence intervals, p-values, and percentage mediation all reported

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal, but social media is IV in primary analysis (not DV-only). EC7 requires DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. Prospective design with strong temporal sequence and mediator pathway. Diverse ABCD sample. Clear operationalization of both social media (time) and outcome (substance use initiation).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=9799, baseline ages 9-10 years, Years 2-3 follow-up data (2018-2020), diverse national cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis using linear and logistic regression to examine associations between cyberbullying victimization (Year 2) and mental health/substance use outcomes (Year 3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Lancet Regional Health Americas 46(2025):101002

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization is the primary exposure variable; no separately-measured smartphone or social media variable appears as IV or DV in the analysis. Per criteria: "Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable) does NOT qualify on FT-IC2."

notes: Excluded at FT-IC2. While the paper reports strong prospective associations between cyberbullying and mental health/substance use outcomes in ABCD, it does not meet the core inclusion criterion requiring a qualifying SM/phone variable as IV or DV.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=10,305, baseline ages 9-10 years at study entry, Year 3 assessment data (2019-2021, ages 12-13 years), 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multiple regression (modified Poisson, multivariable linear, multivariable logistic) to examine sociodemographic associations with bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Sleep Health 11(2025):562-571

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use (IV) measured across multiple isolated modalities: watching movies/videos/TV, video games, music, phone/texting, social media, Internet, computer/laptop. Sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, sleep duration) are individual-trait-level health variables. Methods explicitly isolate phone and social media items as separate bedtime screen modalities.

FT_IC3.evidence: Bedtime screen use mean score associated with sleep disturbance OR=1.36 (95% CI 1.23-1.50, p<0.001); association with sleep duration B=-0.43 (95% CI -0.52 to -0.34, p<0.001). Individual modalities report OR and B with 95% CIs and p-values.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results across outcome measures and stratified analyses: ORs and B coefficients for sleep disturbance and duration, stratified by sex, household income, parental education; all with 95% CIs and p-values

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs. Screen use is IV, sleep is DV. EC7 requirement does not apply.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. Cross-sectional design with large, demographically diverse ABCD sample. Clear operationalization of bedtime screen use with phone and social media isolated from other modalities. Multiple numeric results for SM/phone-sleep associations. Sociodemographic stratification demonstrates differential effects.
